Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd tell myself I'd study on certain days, but then I'd just... not feel like it. Most of my studying happened in random bursts, usually late at night when I couldn't sleep or when anxiety about deadlines kicked in. I know I'm supposed to be more organized, but everything just feels so monotonous that I can't bring myself to stick to a schedule.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without opening anything course-related, then suddenly cram when I realized something was due. Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. Regular times? That sounds nice in theory, but my motivation just doesn't work that way.
Q3: Usually panic about a deadline or getting an email notification that something was due soon. Sometimes I'd log in just to scroll through and see what I was missing, which honestly made me feel worse. Occasionally boredom would push me there, like I'd think maybe this time it would be interesting, but it rarely was.
Q4: For a few weeks early on, I tried making it a habit to check the LMS every morning with my coffee. It kind of worked for a bit, but then the content started feeling repetitive and I lost interest. The habit faded pretty quickly once the novelty wore off and I realized I was just going through the motions.
Q5: The video lectures were actually helpful when I could focus on them. I'm a visual learner, so seeing diagrams and demonstrations made concepts click better than reading walls of text. The problem is staying engaged long enough to actually watch them all the way through without my mind wandering.
Q6: The discussion forums felt pointless to me most of the time. I'd post occasionally because we had to, but reading through everyone's responses felt like a chore. The readings I often skipped entirely or skimmed at best. If it wasn't visually engaging, I probably wasn't going to absorb it anyway.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate relevance or if it was actually interesting. When something connected to real-world applications or had good visuals, I'd engage more. But most things felt like busywork designed to fill time rather than actually teach me something meaningful.
Q8: Optional activities might as well not exist for me. If something isn't required, my brain immediately categorizes it as 'skip.' Required activities at least had the pressure of grades behind them, which was sometimes the only thing getting me to do anything. Without that external push, I just drift.
Q9: Yeah, I downloaded a lot of PDFs and videos to my laptop. I'd watch lectures on my tablet in bed or while doing other things. I also used YouTube videos and other websites when the course materials weren't clicking. A lot of my actual learning happened outside the LMS, just in more comfortable settings.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours going through downloaded materials or rewatching videos I'd saved, but none of that showed up anywhere. I'd also study with friends sometimes, working through problems together, but that's invisible to the system. My LMS activity definitely doesn't reflect all the time I actually spent.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download lecture slides or videos once and then never go back to the LMS for them. I preferred having everything saved locally so I could access it without logging in or dealing with slow loading times. It was just easier to study from my own files.
Q12: Around midterms, everything felt impossible. I was stressed about multiple courses, my anxiety was through the roof, and I just couldn't bring myself to care about this course specifically. The content started blurring together and I'd open the LMS, stare at it, and then close it without doing anything.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed with other classes and life stuff, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. I'd tell myself I'd catch up later, but later never really came. Fatigue made everything feel harder than it needed to be, and I'd just zone out instead of engaging.
Q14: So many times. I'd have a video playing but be scrolling on my phone or thinking about something else entirely. I'd click through slides without actually reading them. Technically I was 'accessing materials,' but my brain was somewhere else. It was like going through the motions without actually being present.
Q15: All the time. I'd know an assignment was important and still put it off because the thought of starting made me anxious. The longer I waited, the worse the anxiety got, which made me avoid it even more. It's a terrible cycle and I know it's self-destructive, but breaking out of it feels impossible sometimes.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd ignore everything until a deadline was looming, then scramble to get things done at the last minute. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have submitted anything. They created stress but also the only structure I actually followed.
Q17: When I got feedback that was encouraging, it helped a little with my mood, but it didn't really change my behavior much. Negative feedback just made me feel worse and less likely to engage. I appreciated when instructors gave specific suggestions, but I rarely followed through on implementing them.
Q18: The course having so many online components meant I could easily fall behind without anyone noticing. There was no one checking in on me, so drifting was easy. The flexibility that was supposed to help actually made it easier for me to disengage because there was no external accountability.
Q19: Having seven online courses simultaneously made everything blur together. The LMS interface wasn't particularly inspiring either—everything looked the same and felt tedious. If the course had more interactive elements or varied content formats, maybe I would have stayed more engaged instead of getting bored.
Q20: That sounds like me sometimes. I'd log in frequently, click around, watch bits of videos, but then never actually complete assignments. It's like window shopping for education—you're there, you're looking, but you're not actually buying anything. The engagement looks high but the follow-through isn't there.
Q21: I think some people are just more efficient or they study in ways that don't show up online. Maybe they're using textbooks or study groups or they just absorb information faster. I managed decent grades despite my low attendance because I could cram effectively when I absolutely had to.
Q22: Logs can show when you clicked things and for how long, but they can't show whether you were actually paying attention or just had a tab open. They miss all the offline studying, the mental engagement, the understanding. They're measuring presence, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it, not just going through motions to get a grade. It's when the content captures your attention and you're genuinely curious. I rarely felt that this semester—mostly I was just trying to survive and pass.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with anxiety about the course, or how bored and disconnected I felt most of the time. It wouldn't capture the CBT sessions I did to try to cope, or how my mood affected my ability to focus. The emotional reality of being a student is completely invisible in the data.
